review:
"You play for a lifetime, you learn for a lifetime" – I used to think this quote was about university, but it turns out it's about mastering Mirage.

As a beginner, I’m mostly learning two things: how to mute the chat at lightning speed and how to spectacularly flash myself with my own grenade. I thought I had decent reflexes until I met a 12-year-old with an AWP who sent me back to the lobby before I could even blink.

This game is incredibly frustrating, the subtick system hates me, and the smokes seem to have a mind of their own. But then, you land that one lucky headshot... and that "dink" sound makes you forget all the humiliation instantly.

review:
👍 Recommended

CS2 is a fantastic free-to-play experience where 10 people join a server and only 2 of them are actually playing the same game.

The gunplay feels crisp, movement is smooth, and every match offers a surprise mechanic called **“Was that skill or software?”**

Premier mode is especially exciting because your rank depends on:
• aim
• teamwork
• luck
• whether the spinning scout on the enemy team gets tired

VAC remains one of gaming’s most beloved decorative features. It adds a comforting sense of tradition without disrupting anyone’s evening.

Still, nothing matches the thrill of hitting a clean headshot, clutching a 1v3, or convincing yourself the enemy was just “having a good day.”

Would recommend to:
✔ people who enjoy competition
✔ people who enjoy suffering
✔ people who enjoy both simultaneously

10/10 Stockholm syndrome simulator.

review:
3/5 Stars

The game is still there and its okay. Valve should've learned from Back 4 Blood and realised their OG games will be hard to top. CS2 is good but ask players if they would rather play CS2 or CS:GO and I promise the majority would rather play CS:GO.

Current Issues with the game:
-Movement feels floaty and slow (especially compared to CS:GO)
-Weapon Accuracy isnt as good as GO, running and gunning is more promoted
-Delay before throwing nades is slower and more "clunky"
-Peekers-advantage is still more prominent in CS2 than GO
-The AWP is overall worse than GO
-lack of feed back i.e csgo blood came from the bullet holes, cs2 just sprays out the player model sometimes, meaning shots with snipers are hard to tell if u hit them

Theres more and each issue have more that can be said. Valve should have left CS2 in a public access with CS:GO still active. Its taken over 2 and half years for CS2 to even start to feel close to CS:GO.
Secure VAC and 128 tick would've kept 90% of the community happy on CS:GO for years to come, instead we are forced to play this and its driving die hard fans away.

review:
I swear this is one of the most toxic games ever created.

You queue up thinking you’ll have a good match, maybe enjoy some strategy and teamwork… and instead you get blamed for everything within the first 5 minutes. Miss one spell? “GG noob.” Don’t rotate perfectly? “Report mid.” Win your lane but your team feeds? Still your fault somehow.

Every game feels like a mental battle more than an actual match. It’s not just about skill — it’s about surviving your own teammates.

And the crazy part? You keep coming back.

Because when you finally get that one good team, that one perfect game where everything clicks… it reminds you why you started playing in the first place.

10/10 gameplay.
2/10 community.
Still queueing.

review:
최근에 저는 Dota 2를 플레이하기 시작했는데, 정말 재미있으면서도 꽤 어려운 게임이라고 느꼈습니다. 제가 평소에 모바일에서 즐기던 Mobile Legends: Bang Bang이나 League of Legends와 비교하면, Dota 2는 훨씬 더 복잡하고 난이도가 높은 편입니다.

처음에는 다양한 시스템과 메커니즘 때문에 조금 부담스럽게 느껴졌습니다. 영웅의 스킬, 아이템 빌드, 맵 이해도 등 배워야 할 것이 많기 때문입니다. 하지만 바로 그 점이 이 게임을 더욱 흥미롭게 만듭니다. 매 경기마다 발전할 부분이 있고, 전략적으로 생각하도록 계속 도전하게 만듭니다.

특히 제가 가장 즐기는 부분은 새로운 영웅을 배우고 플레이하는 것입니다. 각 영웅은 고유한 특징을 가지고 있어서 익히는 데 시간이 필요하지만, 그만큼 성장하는 과정이 매우 보람 있습니다. 게임 플레이에 적응하는 것은 쉽지 않지만, 그 과정이 오히려 더 큰 재미를 줍니다.

전체적으로 보면, 익숙한 게임들보다 더 어렵지만 저는 Dota 2를 정말 좋아하게 되었습니다. 도전적인 요소와 깊이 있는 시스템, 그리고 계속해서 배울 수 있는 점이 이 게임을 계속 다시 플레이하고 싶게 만듭니다.

review:
The biggest issue with this game is the "brain-rot" mentality of the player base. I once took a long break after being mass-reported and banned for winning a game 27-29*/*/30* as Crystal Maiden. Because I wasn’t following a cookie-cutter guide, my team couldn’t handle the "off-meta" playstyle—even though I practically solo-carried us to a win.

The report system is weaponized to force you into a boring, rigid playstyle. When your unique performance is judged first by a couple of imbeciles in-game, and later by an Overwatch reviewer who lacks the perspective to see past the meta, the game fails. It is far from the "ideal" competitive experience it has a reputation for. It rewards people who have been stuck in the same skill bracket for a decade over those who actually know how to adapt and innovate.

review: I hit “play now” like i always do… hands already a little shaky for no reason 😅 queue pops and my heart starts racing again. you’d think after all those hours in dota i’d be used to it by now… but nah, every game still feels like stepping into something unpredictable, like anything can go wrong at any moment.. last night i went mid… yeah probably not my smartest choice. the guy i was against was insane from the start, hitting everything perfectly like he already knew what i was gonna do. i missed one easy last hit and my brain just started spiraling a bit. but then somehow it shifted. i took one risky move, landed a clean combo… and suddenly the whole game felt different. like everything just clicked for a second. my team started playing better too, or at least it felt like they believed again. that’s what’s crazy about this game. it hits you hard when you mess up, but when you pull something off… it feels unreal, still stressful tho… toxic players, late night games, questioning your life at 2am 😅 but yeah… somehow you still press queue again, would i recommend it? yeah… but only if you’re ready to feel like a genius and a mess in the same game

review:
Hello guys! So I feel like after 7000 hours and many years of changes I now need to leave a honest opinion about this game. So why the dislike? Simple 2 main reasons:
1. The report system is the work of someone who thinks that a small series of code lines can replace the work of humans and if you play enough even if you will find that you will lose points in games you said nothing at all because people report for any reason they think fit. If by any chance you decide you tell another team mate that he did something wrong, or you tell them that they made the wards free and even at Ancient level, there is like 80% chance, that NO ONE buys even vision wards, YOU get reported because you are TOXIC, and they report, and finally you reach a point where you can't use any communication at all and that make the game MORE FUN! Amazing.
2. The AMAZING ROLE QUEUE system. SO in short you CAN play ROLEs but you CAN'T actually play the role you love because AGAIN someone (not giving names here!!) decided to FORCE players on other roles, RANDOM, just so you can play yours from time to time. WHAT THIS DOES is this:
 1. Players on roles they don't want to play select heroes that are not made for that role or don't play the role what so ever. So the chances are you get offlaners that play carry, supports that play carry heroes, no wards, not the items needed. You get the point.
 2.  You are forced in roles and heroes you don't play or LIKE at all and the win rate drops significantly when you RUN OUT of ROLE QUEUES and need to waste time of your life playing something you don't actually like. THAT's the fun you want to waste your time on. :)
So I REALLY HOPE that someone that has some wish to suffer a bit will see this and will use his time in a better manner OR that Valve will try actually to DO SOMETHING better and LOOK at other COMPETITIVE games on the market that ROLES are ROLES for a reason.
THANKS and have a nice one! GL with the players you get and ask yourself if you see an idiot on your team, IF he is forced to TRY new HEROES in RANKED games just cuz DOTA forced them BEFORE YOU REPORT!

With cordiality,
A player

review:
Dota 2 is one of those games that’s easy to try but incredibly hard to master—and that’s exactly what makes it special. Developed by Valve Corporation, it offers one of the deepest competitive experiences I’ve ever played.

Every match feels different. With over 100 heroes, countless item builds, and constantly evolving strategies, no two games are the same. Whether you enjoy outsmarting opponents with strategy or testing your mechanical skills, Dota 2 delivers on both fronts.

The learning curve is definitely steep, but that’s part of the journey. Once things start to click, the game becomes extremely rewarding. Winning a tough match after 40+ minutes of intense gameplay feels unmatched.

The visuals, sound design, and overall polish are top-tier, and the fact that it’s completely free-to-play without pay-to-win mechanics makes it even better.

It’s not just a game—it’s a long-term challenge that keeps pushing you to improve.

Highly recommended if you’re ready to invest time and effort.

review:
I’ve been playing since Warcraft III back in 2006, and I moved to Dota 2 in 2014. I stopped in 2024 and only came back last month but coming back made me realize something I’ve never really said out loud.

When everything in my life starts falling apart, Dota is the one thing that’s always there.

It sounds dramatic for a game, but it’s the truth. There were moments where I didn’t know what to do, where things felt heavy, confusing, or just empty and somehow, queuing up for a match gave me something to hold onto. A reason to stay focused. A way to get through another day.

It’s not perfect. It can be frustrating, toxic, exhausting. But even then, it never left. It gave me structure when I had none, something familiar when everything else felt uncertain.

I’m still here, typing this, because I had something to come back to.

So yeah this isn’t just a game to me. It’s been a constant in a life that hasn’t always been stable.

Thank you for that.

I’m writing this with tears in my eyes and I mean every word.

review:
If I had to sum up my experience with Dota 2 in one sentence: it’s chaos, strategy, stress, and pure satisfaction all rolled into one—and I love every second of it.

This game is on another level when it comes to depth. Every match feels like a chess game where the pieces are constantly on fire. The heroes are insanely unique, the strategy is endless, and no two games ever play out the same. One minute you’re getting absolutely humbled, the next you’re pulling off a comeback that feels like a movie climax.

Yeah, it’s got a steep learning curve—like, borderline vertical—but once it clicks, it really clicks. The teamwork, the mind games, the clutch plays… it hits different. Even the frustration somehow becomes part of the charm.

It’s not just a game, it’s a whole experience. And honestly? I wouldn’t trade it for anything.

review:
If you have played only ranked games in your life you have probably never played a real dota!

Ranked games are full of AI - you get a few of them on your team and sometimes all on the enemy's. Their play is deciding the result of the game. The result is decided through an algorythm which keeps all players in the range of cca 47 - 50 % winrate so it really doesn't matter how you play, even the worst players will end up winning same often like everyone else. The AI is very sophisticated and can beat all human players don't matter how good they are. review:
[h1]Overall Verdict: The Only Battle Royale That Actually Scratches the Itch[/h1]

I came to [i]PUBG[/i] looking for a break from the other "popular" battle royales, and honestly, none of them ever scratched the itch quite like this one. There is a specific kind of intensity here—especially in the final circles where everyone is locked in holding sniper angles—that is just unmatched.

[h2]My Experience[/h2]

At the start, I spent a lot of time just fighting the map itself. I’d get so caught up in the sheer scale of the world that I wouldn't pay attention to the time or the encroaching circle, leading to a lot of preventable deaths. But, once I got the hang of it, that same massive map became a total sniper’s heaven. 

The difficulty curve, however, is wild. I’ve had sessions where I’ve cleared 20+ kills, feeling completely invincible, and other days where I struggled to find even a single kill. The jump in difficulty between lobbies is insane, which makes for a pretty jarring experience sometimes.

[h2]The Good & The Bad[/h2]

[b]Pros:[/b]
[list]
[*] [b]Unrivaled Intensity:[/b] The final circles are genuinely heart-pounding.
[*] [b]Sniper Gameplay:[/b] The map design makes for the best long-range combat in the genre.
[*] [b]Satisfying Highs:[/b] When the game clicks, it is an incredible rush that other BRs just don't deliver.
[/list]

[b]Cons:[/b]
[list]
[*] [b]Matchmaking Inconsistency:[/b] The skill gap feels massive; some days I'm dominating, other days I'm getting stomped immediately.
[*] [b]Mode Fragmentation:[/b] While you can toggle into FPP while playing TPP, it’s frustrating that some modes are strictly locked to FPP only. I really wish there was more flexibility to choose your perspective across all modes.
[*] [b]Confusing UI:[/b] Personally, I really don't like the menu system. It feels confusing, messy, and way too busy.
[*] [b]Ranked Access:[/b] I've never even been able to queue for a ranked game; the mode just doesn't work for me at all.
[*] [b]Technical Concerns:[/b] I’ve definitely had moments where I had to report players for what felt like blatant cheating.
[/list]

[h2]Verdict[/h2]

If you want a BR that feels tactical and rewards patience over pure twitch reflexes, this is the one. It’s flawed, and the matchmaking can be a rollercoaster, but when it’s good, it remains the gold standard for the genre.

review:
7 Years of Loyalty, 0 Days of Optimization
I’ve been playing this game since 2017, right from the day it launched. I’ve stuck with it through every meta and every map, but my patience is finally running out. It’s painful to watch a game with such unique gunplay being slowly killed by corporate greed and poor technical management.

The Reality Check:
Performance & Freezes: It is 2026, and we are still dealing with stuttering, random freezes, and general performance issues. I have a high-end build, yet the game feels like it’s struggling to breathe. Why is optimization always at the bottom of the priority list?

Skins over Substance: Instead of fixing the core mechanics or addressing the long-overdue move to a new Unreal Engine, the developers are obsessed with pumping out "sloppy" skins and ridiculous cosmetics that ruin the game's atmosphere. We wanted a tactical battle royale, not a digital fashion show.

The "Engine" Excuse: We keep hearing how "difficult" it is to transition to a modern engine. Krafton is a multi-billion dollar company. Smaller studios manage to upgrade their tech, but here we are, stuck on an outdated foundation because the focus is strictly on monetization.

Killing the Vibe: The game is losing its identity. Every update brings more "nonsense" content and less stability.

Verdict: If you’re a veteran from 2017, you know exactly what I’m talking about. We love the core of PUBG, but the management is running it into the ground.

Dear Developers: Stop painting dresses and start fixing the engine.


"I cannot recommend this game in its current state. As someone who has been here since 2017, it's heartbreaking to see the devs prioritize K-pop skins and expensive progressive crates over basic game stability. We are still waiting for the UE5 transition while the current client suffers from performance drops and freezes. Great gunplay, terrible management."

review:
I am one of the early players of this game, and I started playing back in 2018. Since then, I have made countless great memories with both my friends and this game itself. Seeing the experience become worse and worse because of hackers truly makes me sad.

The developers do not seem to care enough about properly improving the anti-cheat system. Instead, they created an AI and gave it the responsibility of detecting cheaters. Of course, since it is AI, it cannot perform this task perfectly.

This AI does not understand that normal players naturally have inconsistent performances. There are days when we play extremely well, and there are also days when we play terribly. Unlike cheaters, who usually maintain suspiciously high KDA stats in almost every match, legitimate players will never perform that consistently.

However, it feels like the developers designed the anti-cheat system under the assumption that if an average player usually performs poorly, then suddenly getting many kills in a single match must mean they are cheating. As a result, legitimate players end up receiving 24-hour bans, while countless actual cheaters continue playing freely despite receiving numerous reports.

Please fix the way your anti-cheat system or anti-cheat AI is programmed. Improve it in a smarter and more reasonable way. Stop overusing 24-hour bans. All this does is drive away loyal and passionate players who genuinely love and care about this game.

review:
With 465.6 hours in PUBG: BATTLEGROUNDS, all played with friends, I’ve had more than enough time to experience what this game does best—and where it still struggles.
The realism is one of the strongest aspects. Gunplay feels grounded, and every fight has weight to it. Especially when it comes to sniping, no other game really matches the satisfaction PUBG delivers. Landing a long-range shot or clutching a fight with your squad just hits differently.That said, it’s hard to ignore how clunky the game can feel, even after all these years. Movement, animations, and general responsiveness sometimes feel outdated compared to newer titles, which can be frustrating in intense moments.Despite that, PUBG still has something special. Playing with friends creates unforgettable moments, whether you’re messing around or trying to win seriously. The tension, the pacing, and those high-stakes endgames keep you coming back.I would recommend it if you enjoy realistic battle royale games and especially if you have friends to play with. Just be prepared to deal with some clunkiness along the way.

review:
I haven't played this game in a such a long time and thought to give a shot after x amount of years. This game used to be so fun for me because I liked how slower paced it was and the gunplay felt satisfying. But going back now was atrocious. 

Well first, I missed the loot boxes that you would get frequently and get random clothing cosmetics, but now they rely on you buying your way into the boxes. You can still get free boxes, but you mostly just get points from them so you can save up and buy ugly and limited cosmetic choices in the store. If you decide to buy an overpriced lootbox, you need to buy keys too. I absolutely hate this system, and I personally think the high-tier cosmetics are awful to begin with. They definitely took inspiration from Fortnite and other battle royales to make some non-grounded outfits and weapons skins that look like they came from space. The store overall is just one of the worse I've seen. 

Second, I hate the player encounters in this. With the player base continuously playing all these years it became very unfun very quickly. These players shoot up to level 1,000+ and know all the ins and outs of the game and absolutely slap me around without a proper chance. Not sure how I can gain more experience to keep up of these players if I'm getting decked at every situation. You can laugh and call me bad, but me against one or two guys at level 1,000+; what am I supposed to do?

Third, I would go into the bot lobbies to gain more familiarity of the game and would be fun but they only allow two to three games per day. Do a couple of games, then they force you to go into full player lobbies to get destroyed and it just loses my motivation to play the game completely. 

Fourth, I hate any usage of AI in video games. Demonstrates poor quality, laziness, and loses any trust from me that they'd stoop that low.
auth_reason: Detailed and coherent review with specific criticisms and personal experience, indicating a genuine player's perspective.

review:
If you had told me in 2018 that I would spend the next eight years of my life getting emotionally attached to a cast-iron skillet, I would have asked you to seek professional help. Yet, here we are in 2026, and my relationship with PUBG: Battlegrounds is the most stable thing in my life.

The Nostalgia is Real
I remember the "Early Days"—back when the car physics were less "vehicle simulation" and more "angry poltergeist." You haven't truly lived until you’ve driven a UAZ over a pebble and been launched into the stratosphere like a budget SpaceX program. We’ve come a long way from those glorious, glitchy roots, but that unpredictable soul is still there, tucked away in every blades-of-grass-filled final circle.

Why I’m Still Dropping (and Dying):
The Cardio: My character has run enough laps around Erangel to qualify for the Olympic marathon. Who needs a gym membership when you have the "Blue Zone" screaming at your heels?

The Gourmet Combat: Where else can a kitchen utensil stop a .50 caliber sniper round? The Pan isn't just a melee weapon; it’s a lifestyle choice. It’s a shield, a symbol of hope, and occasionally, a dinner bell for my own demise.

The Fashion: I’ve evolved from a guy in a dirty t-shirt to a tactical wasteland warrior wearing a dinosaur suit and neon-pink goggles. It’s called intimidation, look it up.

The Adrenaline: No other game captures that specific brand of "heart-attack-inducing" silence. You’ll spend fifteen minutes loot-vacuuming a city in total peace, only to have a single floorboard creak turn the game into a high-octane horror movie.

review: Few games still create panic as well as PUBG. You can loot for ten quiet minutes, feel completely in control, then hear one distant shot and suddenly start moving like your real life depends on a helmet and a painkiller. That tension is why it still works. Gunfights feel heavy, mistakes feel expensive, and every match has that “one bad second and it’s over” energy. The funny part is how fast confidence disappears. Everyone talks like a tactician in the lobby, then the circle closes, a car flips, somebody misses the easiest spray ever, and the squad turns into pure stress. But that is also what makes wins feel so good. It is not just about shooting well, it is about surviving your own bad decisions long enough to look smart at the end. PUBG is still one of the few battle royale games where even hiding in a bush can feel dramatic. Slow when it wants to be, brutal when it needs to be, and somehow always one match away from dragging you back in.

review:
PUBG: Battlegrounds — 9/10
Before Fortnite. Before Warzone. Before any of them. There was PUBG, and it changed everything.
Say what you want about the state of battle royale today — every single one of those games owes its existence to what PUBG built from the ground up. The 100 players dropping on an island, the shrinking circle, the looting, the tension of the final ring — PUBG invented the template that an entire genre just copy-pasted and dressed up differently. This is where it all started.
And honestly? It still plays better than most of its imitators. There's a weight and grittiness to PUBG that nobody has quite managed to replicate. The gunplay feels real and punishing. Bullets have travel time and drop. You can't just spray and pray — you have to think about range, positioning, cover, vehicle sounds in the distance, footsteps on the floor above you. Every engagement feels genuinely tense in a way that flashier battle royales rarely achieve.
Erangel is still one of the best maps ever designed for the genre. Open fields, dense towns, chokepoints, rivers — it forces every kind of combat and never feels unfair. The other maps each bring something different to the table and after all these years there's still a ridiculous amount of variety.
Yes it had a rough early life. Yes the optimization has been a journey. But as a pure, unfiltered battle royale experience built on real tactical foundations, nothing touches the original.
The genre grew up and moved on. But the peak? It was always here.
9/10 — The original. The blueprint. Still the best.

review:
Imagine spending 20 minutes playing "Interior Designer" in a derelict Russian shed, meticulously collecting a Level 3 backpack and a suppressed M416, only to be erased from existence by a guy laying in a bush with a crossbow.

Why You'll Hate It:
The Physics: Vehicles behave like they were programmed by someone who has only ever heard physics described over a bad phone line. Hit a pebble? Your car is now a satellite.

The Combat: You don't "fight." You loiter in a bathroom for ten minutes until the Blue Zone—the game's personification of tax season—forces you out to get shot by a 12-year-old with the reflexes of a cobra on meth.

The Optimization: It runs like a brick through a blender. Your PC will scream in agony just to render a texture that looks like wet cardboard.

The Verdict:
It’s a toxic cycle of looting, running, and dying to things you can't see. It’s miserable, unfair, and technically broken.

I'll see you in the lobby in five minutes.

review:
The game is rough… the cheaters suck on here for sure, and every good server seems to wipe every 3 days. BUT when you find a good server and you’re actually grinding and I don’t mean no-lifing, just a normal grind this game shines.

If you come from Escape from Tarkov and you’re chasing the anxiety it gives you every time you load in… this is better lol.

At first, I hated Rust. Just a bunch of little kids yelling slurs while taking your stuff. Nah… imagine grinding for hours, having everything you could need, and then having to defend it all against someone with thousands of hours.

Now imagine winning.

Although it’s a small chance, when it happens it’s absolutely exhilarating, so much so that it can honestly become addictive. That rush of putting everything on the line, knowing you could lose it all… just for some kid who’s never even heard the word “job” to show up with 10 of his friends and take everything haha…

I didn’t start enjoying it until I was over 200 hours in, so I guess give it a chance if you’re interested.

review:
I’m not even reviewing the gameplay here, I’m reviewing how this game treats its players.

My account was compromised, and during that time someone else played on it and triggered a ban. The timeline is extremely clear: I had only a few minutes of playtime total, then suddenly there was a spike of over 10 hours during the exact period my account was compromised, which is also when the ban happened.

I secured my account immediately, changed everything, and provided support with the full context and timeline. There have been no issues since.

Despite this, support refused to review the situation in any meaningful way and upheld the ban purely based on policy.

So to be clear:

* They can see abnormal activity
* They can see a clear discrepancy in playtime
* They can see it was a one-time event

…and they still choose to treat legitimate players the same as actual cheaters.

I fully support anti-cheat systems, but punishing players for confirmed account compromises with no room for review is a terrible system.

If something happens to your account, don’t expect any help. You’ll just be told “policy” and that’s the end of it.
auth_reason: Detailed account of a specific incident with clear narrative and technical details, consistent with a genuine user experience.

Cheaters everywhere.

* Even if you build a huge base, they can take over your base by destroying just one wall and one door. they are able to see inside the base using the noclip cheat. And there's probably a system that displays objects so they can see the boxes and the Tool Cupboard

* You could die from shots that happen in milliseconds, all within the same pattern. There's no mechanism to detect it.

* Cheaters dominate the areas where events take place.

* Even from far away, cheaters hide among the trees and sneak up behind you. Also they generally prefer to go "offline raid" and leave without killing you.

* The game mechanics prevent firing in the air, but cheaters can bypass this.

* When you're hiding somewhere, someone can just come towards you as if nothing is there and kill you immediately.

It demands every possible effort and takes no precautions against cheaters.

"I started playing this game for a 'relaxing survival experience.' 10 minutes in, I was chased down a beach by four naked men screaming into their mics in languages I didn’t know existed, only to be beaten to death with a rock while a child called me every slur in the dictionary.

I spent the next 18 hours building a 2x1 wooden shack. I felt like a king. I went to the kitchen to make a piece of toast, came back 4 minutes later, and there were 3 guys with Kalashnikovs standing over my corpse. They didn't even take my loot—they just changed the locks on my front door and played 'Baby Shark' through my own speakers while I watched from the death screen.

This game didn't just test my survival skills; it gave me permanent psychological damage and a deep-seated distrust of anyone holding a torch. I have lost my job, my wife left me because I was 'defending the compound' during her sister’s wedding, and I haven't seen sunlight since the experimental branch. My sleep schedule is now dictated by the raid habits of a Russian zerg clan.

I woke up on the beach today and saw a naked man hitting a cactus with a bone club while crying. I walked up and bashed his head in with a rock just so I could feel something again.

Rust is the best video game I absolutely refuse to play. It is a path to pure nihilism and a mirror of everything wrong with humanity.

10/10. Would play again until my heart gives out."

review:
Rust is one of those games that sticks with you—whether you love it, hate it, or somehow both at the same time.

From the moment you spawn in with nothing but a rock and a torch, the game throws you into a brutal, unpredictable world where every decision matters. It’s not just about survival against the environment, but against other players—and that’s where Rust truly shines. The tension of not knowing whether someone will help you or betray you keeps every encounter intense and memorable.

What makes Rust so addictive is the progression. Starting from absolutely nothing and slowly building up a base, gathering resources, crafting weapons, and defending what’s yours feels incredibly rewarding. At the same time, it can all be taken away in an instant through raids, which makes every success feel earned.

The community can be chaotic, toxic, hilarious, and surprisingly cooperative all at once. Some of the best moments come from completely random interactions—forming alliances, getting into fights, or just messing around with strangers.

Even now, with the game having been out for years, it still feels alive thanks to constant updates and an active player base. There’s always something new to try, whether it’s different servers, playstyles, or strategies.

Rust isn’t for everyone—it can be unforgiving and frustrating—but if you enjoy high-risk, high-reward gameplay and creating your own stories, it’s an experience unlike any other. It’s the kind of game where no two sessions are ever the same, and that’s what keeps me coming back.

review:
If you're thinking about getting into Rust, the first thing I'll say is, "don't, it'll ruin your life." If you're absolutely sure, then here's what I have to say. 
  It's a good game, it's very addicting once you learn it and know what's going on. I would advise playing on a 2x or 3x times solo/duo only modded server with a medium pop that wipes once or twice a week. 
  This way you avoid the hardcore grinding for 3 weeks to get raided by some clan of 50 dudes in their moms basement. You can throw away a weekend on a 2x solo only server and have fun and then do it again next time you're up for it. 
Do not go on the official vanilla servers that wipe once a month unless you've got a month to spare and know a few (min 5+ people) that are also willing to spare a month of their life.
Trust nobody.
Good game overall, can def take over your life though.

review:
Bought Rust thinking it was a survival game. It is not. It is a violent social experiment where naked people with rocks commit war crimes over a wooden door.

You spawn on a beach with a torch and a dream, then immediately get chased by a man named ToeSniffer69 screaming into his mic while hitting you with a rock.

I spent six hours farming wood to build a base that looked like a refrigerator box. A guy flew over in a helicopter, called it “cute,” and blew my house into another zip code.

Every “friendly” player is lying. If someone says “I’m friendly,” prepare to wake up on the beach with no pants.

I made allies once. We became brothers. We defended the base together. We laughed. We shared food. Then they changed the codes and stole my furnace.

This game turns normal people into paranoid raccoons. I hear footsteps in real life and want to upgrade my walls.

You don’t sleep in Rust. You log off and pray.

My daily routine:
Wake up.
Hit tree.
Get shot.
Respawn.
Hit tree again.
Get called trash by a child.
Repeat.

I spent three days collecting guns just to lose them because I opened the wrong door and let in a naked guy with a spear who somehow had the combat skills of a Navy SEAL.

The animals are demons.
Boars hate you.
Bears want your bloodline ended.
Scientists have better aim than most military units.

And the players?
A 13 year old with 4,000 hours will hear one twig snap and raid your entire bloodline.

Rust doesn’t have beginners.
It has victims.

This game has made me experience emotions I didn’t know existed:
Fear.
Rage.
Betrayal.
Starvation.
Roof campers.

10/10 masterpiece.
Would absolutely spend eight hours farming sulfur just to get offline raided while eating dinner again.

review:
**THEY ARE DESTROYING MY LIFE AND MY PASSION**
I have dedicated **3,445 hours** of my life to Rust. This wasn't just a game to me; it was my escape, my community, and my passion. Today, I am left with nothing but deep depression and thoughts of ending it all because of a massive injustice by Facepunch and Easy Anti-Cheat (EAC).
I was hit with a **FALSE BAN** caused by a confirmed technical conflict with **Malwarebytes antivirus**. I have official, written proof from Malwarebytes (Ticket #8346625) admitting the error. But when I show this proof to the developers, they treat me like a criminal. They play "human volleyball" with my life—Facepunch tells me to go to EAC, EAC sends me to a broken automated website, and Epic Games Support ignores me.
**I have lost over $200 worth of skins and all my DLCs.** All that hard-earned money and time, gone in a second. But the money isn't what hurts the most. It’s the humiliation. My friends are mocking me, thinking I’m a cheater when I’ve never cheated in my life. I’ve lost my reputation and my friends.
I am now struggling with **severe depression**. This game, which I loved so much, has turned its back on me and treated me with zero humanity. They won't even let me speak to a real person. They are happy to take our money for skins and DLCs, but when their system makes a mistake, they throw us away like trash.
If you value your mental health and your money, **STAY AWAY FROM THIS GAME.** They will rob you of your time, your money, and your will to live, and they will never say sorry.
**PLEASE, SOMEONE AT FACEPUNCH, LOOK AT MY CASE BEFORE IT'S TOO LATE.**

review:
Do you hate your life? Do you have hours of time to dedicate to an experience that ultimately leaves you miserable? Do you hate games that are actually optimized and run well? Do you despise people with a functioning frontal lobe and kindness in their hearts? 

Rust is the game for you. 

Never have I wanted to enjoy a game more than Rust. The mechanics are interesting, base building is fun, there's so much you can do with electricity and automation. Survival is calm--grindy, but enjoyable. Then the fact that this game is multiplayer rears its ugly head. The problem with Rust is that the point of playing it is to defend against other players, or to attack them. There is no way out of this inherently unfriendly system. You will watch everything you did be erased, over and over, either by a wipe, a raid, or your own motivation finally running out, and decay taking course. (PvE servers exist but by removing the inherent motivation of being attacked there is no reason to do anything) Not to mention the only people who still regularly play this game have 1,000+ hours and no job besides Rust.

Rust is also terribly unoptimized. I have played the game on three pc's now, each better than the last, and it still runs like garbage. And god forbid you have an internet browser open, or are watching a video. I have my pc hard reset to memory loss countless times. On the worst possible graphics. I can play Cyberpunk 2077 with raytracing at 120fps but I can't run this game above maybe 50. 

Finally, the people. Rust is a cesspool of the worst humanity has to offer. Give kindness and you will only be taken advantage of. The chat is a mindless place where idiots argue ad infinitum. Clans dominate almost every server with nothing better to do than watch the game 24/7. 

TLDR, if you love yourself, stay clear of this pile of crap. You deserve better.

review:
Это просто какой-то ад на земле 🤯. Одна из тех игр, которые заставляют тебя возненавидеть человечество.

С первых минут тебя встречает пустынный, унылый мир 🌿🔫. Выживание, где голые мужики с камнями бегают по одинаковым серым скалам и гнилым лесам — это выглядит как худшая версия постапокалипсиса. Графика унылая и мыльная, даже на максималках кажется, что игра 2012 года 🖼️🤮.

Игроки — отдельная песня! 🔥🤬 Они не смелые и не харизматичные. Это стаи отморозков на вертолётах, которые убивают тебя за копеечный лут, пока ты только-только скрафтил каменный топор. За историей своего персонажа ты следишь как за трешем: родился → нашёл гриб → умер от голода → тебя добил сосед → очнулся в рандомном месте. Сюжета нет. Совсем. Просто песочница для токсиков 🏛️➡️💩.

Отдельный "кайф" — бои! 🏹 Бегать по пустоши, надеясь, что 12-летний задрот в броне не заметит тебя из снайперки, ставить ловушки, которые всё равно сломают через час, и пытаться отстреливаться из ржавого револьвера против автоматов — это дарит такой уровень стресса, что хочется разбить клавиатуру. А когда ты наконец построил хату из дерева, а утром заходишь, а там от неё только фундамент... это просто детская травма! 🦖💔

Музыка? Её почти нет. Атмосфера — токсичность и гринд. Прокачка? Всё сбрасывается при вайпе. Охота — на нубов с камнями. Сделано с душой? Сделано с душком. 3/10. Давно я не получала столько злости, бессонницы и желания удалить игру к чертям. Советую только мазохистам и тем, у кого нет чувства собственного достоинства 😈🖱️
